title: Anger Thought Record
description: Use this worksheet to examine and challenge the thoughts that fuel your anger. The goal isn't to talk yourself out of being angry — it's to check whether your interpretation is accurate and proportionate, and to find a more balanced perspective that's firm but fair.
instructions: When you notice anger rising (ideally before it peaks), work through each step below. If you can't do it in the moment, fill it in as soon afterward as possible — even a few hours later is valuable.

fields.label: 1. What happened? (Stick to facts — who, what, when, where)
fields.placeholder: e.g., My coworker took credit for my idea in the team meeting in front of our manager

fields.label: 2. The hot thought — What went through your mind?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'They always steal my work. They have no respect for me. They're trying to sabotage me.'

fields.label: 3. How angry are you? (0 = not at all, 100 = most angry ever)
4. Which anger-fuelling pattern is at work?

options.label: Should Statements — 'They should know better / must behave differently'
Mind-Reading — 'They did this on purpose / They intended to hurt me'
Catastrophizing — 'This is unbearable / Everything is ruined'
Labeling — Reducing someone to a single judgment ('idiot', 'selfish')
Personalizing — 'This is about me / They're targeting me specifically'

fields.label: 5. Evidence for this thought — What supports it?
fields.placeholder: e.g., They did present my idea without mentioning me. This has happened once before with a different project.

fields.label: 6. Evidence against / Alternative explanations — What else could be true?
fields.placeholder: e.g., They may not have realized it was my idea originally. They've credited me in other situations. I didn't speak up in the meeting when I had the chance.

fields.label: 7. Balanced thought — Firm but fair. What's a more realistic perspective?
fields.placeholder: e.g., It's possible they didn't do it intentionally, but I still need to address it. I'll talk to them privately and make sure I get credit. If it keeps happening, I'll escalate it.

fields.label: 8. How angry are you now? (0 = not at all, 100 = most angry ever)

distortionsList.name: Should Statements
distortionsList.description: Rigid rules about how other people must behave. When reality doesn't match the 'should,' it feels like a moral violation. Replace 'should' with 'I'd prefer' to reduce the intensity.

distortionsList.name: Mind-Reading
distortionsList.description: Assuming you know someone's motives — and assuming they're hostile. 'He did that to embarrass me.' You're often wrong about others' intentions. Ask: is there another explanation?

distortionsList.name: Catastrophizing
distortionsList.description: Turning one frustrating event into a disaster. 'I can't stand this.' 'Everything is ruined.' Check: is this really as bad as it feels right now, or is the anger amplifying it?

distortionsList.name: Overgeneralizing
distortionsList.description: Using 'always,' 'never,' 'nobody,' 'everyone' to turn one event into a permanent pattern. 'You never listen.' Be specific: 'You didn't listen just now' is more accurate and less inflammatory.

distortionsList.name: Labeling
distortionsList.description: Reducing a whole person to a single negative judgment: 'idiot,' 'selfish,' 'worthless.' Separating the person from the behavior ('that was a careless thing to do') keeps anger proportionate.

distortionsList.name: Personalizing
distortionsList.description: Assuming that someone's behavior is directed at you specifically when it may not be. 'They did that to spite me.' Consider: could this have nothing to do with me?

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational self-reflection, not a substitute for professional therapy. If your anger involves violence, threats, or harm, seek professional help immediately. If you are in crisis, call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline).
